content.problemStatement:
You are given two integers, nnn and iii. Your task is to determine whether the **ithi^{th}ith bit** (0-indexed from the least significant bit) in the **binary form of n** is 111.
Return truetruetrue if the bit is set, otherwise return falsefalsefalse.

Function DeclarationFunction NameisBitSet\text{isBitSet}isBitSet – This function checks whether the ithi^{th}ith bit (0-indexed from the least significant bit) in the binary representation of an integer nnn is set.

Parameters
nnn: An integer whose binary representation is to be examined.
iii: An integer indicating the bit position (0-indexed from the least significant bit).
Return Value
Returns truetruetrue if the ithi^{th}ith bit of nnn is set (i.e., equals 1).
Returns falsefalsefalse otherwise.
`The input and output formats given below are only if you want to test using custom inputs.`

Constraints
1≤T≤1051 \leq T \leq 10^51≤T≤105
1≤n≤3∗1091 \leq n \leq 3*10^91≤n≤3∗109
0≤i≤310 \leq i \leq 310≤i≤31

Input Format


The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.




Each test case consists of two lines containing integers:



nnn — the number
iii — the bit position to check




Output Format

For each test case, print truetruetrue if the ithi^{th}ith bit of nnn is set.
Otherwise, print falsefalsefalse.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two integers n and i (separated by space or newline). n is the number whose binary representation is examined, i is the 0‑indexed bit position to check.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "true" if the i‑th bit of n is set (equals 1), otherwise output "false".

sampleTestCases.explanation: 12 in binary is 1100. The bit at index 2 (counting from the right, 0‑based) is 1, so the answer is true.
7 in binary is 0111. The bit at index 3 is 0, therefore the answer is false.
20 in binary is 10100. The bit at index 4 is 1, so the answer is true.

solution.approach: For each test case we need to know whether the i‑th bit (0‑indexed from the least‑significant side) of n is 1. This can be done with a single bitwise operation: shift n right by i positions and check the least‑significant bit, or equivalently test (n & (1LL << i)). If the result is non‑zero the bit is set. The algorithm runs in O(1) per query, so total time is O(T) and only O(1) extra memory is used.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each of the T test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are stored regardless of input size.

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        n = int(next(it))
        i = int(next(it))
        out.append('true' if (n >> i) & 1 else 'false')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
